coredata.dc:title: A dual emissive silver-riboflavin complex and nitrogen-doped carbon dot nanoprobe for ratiometric detection of glutathione
coredata.dc:creator: Alhazzani, Khalid
Alanazi, Ahmed Z.
Mostafa, Aya M.
Barker, James
El-Wekil, Mohamed M.
Ali, Al-Montaser Bellah H.
coredata.dc:description: Glutathione (GSH) is an important antioxidant biomarker with pivotal roles in multiple biological processes. Herein, a novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe was developed for selective and sensitive GSH detection by exploiting competitive interactions with a silver-riboflavin (Ag-RF) complex. The nanoprobe consists simply of a mixed solution of Ag-RF complex displaying characteristic yellow emission at 525 nm and nitrogen-doped carbon dots exhibiting blue emission at 440 nm. Due to the strong affinity of GSH toward silver ions, the addition of GSH liberates riboflavin from the Ag-RF complex leading to quenching of adjacent nitrogen-doped carbon dots through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). By ratiometrically measuring the fluorescence intensities at 525 and 440 nm (F525/F440), GSH levels can be rapidly quantified without interference. This sensor gave excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9986) over 0.05–70 μmol/L GSH with high sensitivity (limit of detection = 0.015 μmol/L). The sensor also provided accurate GSH analysis in spiked human serum samples (96–98.5 % recoveries), validating practical applicability. With advantages of simplicity, selectivity, and low sample volume requirements, this fluorescent nanoprobe holds great promise as a tool for real-time, in-field monitoring of GSH for basic research and clinical investigations.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Glutathione
Dual-emission
Ratiometric
Silver-riboflavin
Carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing of GSH in human serum samples
Morphological and structural characterization of N-CDs
Optical characterization of N-CDs and Ag-RF complex
Effects of experimental conditions on GSH detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2023R593) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image (inset is high resolution TEM), (B) XRD pattern, and (C) FT-IR spectrum of N-CDs.

(A) UV–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation, and emission spectra of the N-CDs. Inset displays photographs under daylight and 365 nm UV illumination. (B) Emission spectra of the N-CDs at excitation wavelengths from 340 to 430 nm. (C) UV–vis absorption spectra for RF and Ag-RF complex. Inset shows their images under visible light. (D) Excitation and Fluorescence emission spectra of Ag-RF.
(A) The effect of variation of concentrations of GSH (0.05–70.0 μmol/L) on the emission intensity of Ag-RF/N-CD. (B) Relationship of (F525/F440)againstconcentration of GSH.
The effect of (A) different amino acids, glucose, and uric acid, (B) metal ions and anions on the fluorescence intensity ratio (F525/F440) of Ag-RF/N-CD system.

Spectral overlay of emission spectra of N-CDs and UV absorption spectra of Ag-RF, Ag-RF + 10 μmol/L GSH, Ag-RF + 30 μmol/L GSH and RF solutions (inset photographs of Ag-RF, Ag-RF + 10 μmol/L GSH, Ag-RF + 30 μmol/L GSH and RF solutions taken under visible light).
Comparison of this work with other reported fluorescent probes for the detection of GSH.

row.entry: Fetal bovine serum and urine samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analytical results of GSH recovery in human serum samples (n = 3).

head.ce:title: A dual emissive silver-riboflavin complex and nitrogen-doped carbon dot nanoprobe for ratiometric detection of glutathione

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmacology and Toxicology, College of Pharmacy, King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, United Kingdom

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Life Sciences, Pharmacy, and Chemistry, Kingston University, Kingston-upon-Thames, London KT1 2EE, United Kingdom

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Assiut University, Assiut, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Glutathione (GSH) is an important antioxidant biomarker with pivotal roles in multiple biological processes. Herein, a novel dual-emission ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe was developed for selective and sensitive GSH detection by exploiting competitive interactions with a silver-riboflavin (Ag-RF) complex. The nanoprobe consists simply of a mixed solution of Ag-RF complex displaying characteristic yellow emission at 525 nm and nitrogen-doped carbon dots exhibiting blue emission at 440 nm. Due to the strong affinity of GSH toward silver ions, the addition of GSH liberates riboflavin from the Ag-RF complex leading to quenching of adjacent nitrogen-doped carbon dots through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). By ratiometrically measuring the fluorescence intensities at 525 and 440 nm (F525/F440), GSH levels can be rapidly quantified without interference. This sensor gave excellent linearity (R2 = 0.9986) over 0.05–70 μmol/L GSH with high sensitivity (limit of detection = 0.015 μmol/L). The sensor also provided accurate GSH analysis in spiked human serum samples (96–98.5 % recoveries), validating practical applicability. With advantages of simplicity, selectivity, and low sample volume requirements, this fluorescent nanoprobe holds great promise as a tool for real-time, in-field monitoring of GSH for basic research and clinical investigations.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Glutathione (GSH) is a crucial tripeptide molecule consisting of glutamate, cysteine, and glycine amino acids. As the most abundant protein thiol, it plays critical biochemical roles in biological systems [1,2]. Under normal physiological conditions, GSH is present at millimolar concentrations intracellularly (1–10 mmol/L) and at micromolar levels in blood serum (1–10 μmol/L) [3,4]. High levels of GSH in the blood may suggest liver disease, oxidative stress, specific cancers, or genetic disorders affecting GSH metabolism [5]. Monitoring GSH levels can assist in the diagnosis, management, and treatment of these conditions, providing valuable insights into a patient's health status. The importance of GSH in biological systems cannot be overstated [6]. As a potent antioxidant, it safeguards cells from damage caused by reactive oxygen species and free radicals. GSH also plays a critical role in detoxifying and eliminating harmful substances, including heavy metals, pollutants, and drugs [6]. Additionally, it supports immune function, regulates gene expression, and maintains cellular energy metabolism. Given the significant role of GSH in health and disease, there is an increasing need for the development of reliable and accurate analytical methods to determine its levels. These methods should enable precise and selective measurement of GSH concentrations in biological samples such as blood, urine, and tissues. Developing such analytical methods is vital for diagnostic purposes, therapeutic monitoring, and research investigations seeking to understand the intricate involvement of GSH in various physiological and pathological processes. GSH has been determined using various analytical techniques such as electrochemical [7,8], spectrophotometric [9], spectrofluorometric [10,11], and high-performance liquid chromatographic methods [12,13].

Riboflavin (RF), a water-soluble vitamin, is well-known for its fluorescence properties, making it a valuable probe in fluorescence studies [14]. It can be effectively utilized as a fluorescent probe, emitting fluorescence at a specific wavelength of 525 nm. The interaction between silver ions and RF, referred to as the silver-RF complex, has been the subject of considerable research reported in different papers [15–19]. This complex exhibits a notable quenching effect on the fluorescence emission of RF. When silver ions are present, they interact with RF molecules, leading to a reduction in the intensity of RF's fluorescence. This quenching phenomenon is attributed to the binding or complex formation between the silver ions and RF [15].

Carbon dots (CDs) are a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials that have attracted great interest in recent years due to their diverse applications. Carbon dots are quasi-spherical nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size [20]. Since their discovery in 2004, carbon dots have been extensively studied due to their high photostability, low toxicity, and excellent biocompatibility [21]. CDs have found uses in a wide range of applications including sensing, bioimaging, drug delivery, photocatalysis, and optoelectronics [21]. Their high fluorescence intensity allows CDs to be used as fluorescent probes for bioimaging applications [22]. CDs can be used to label and image cells and tissues, making them a promising alternative to traditional semiconductor quantum dots [23]. CDs have been successfully employed in various biosensing platforms, including the detection of biomolecules such as glucose [24], DNA [25], and proteins [26–28]. There are several common methods used to synthesize carbon dots, such as hydrothermal synthesis, laser ablation, electrochemical oxidation, and microwave assisted synthesis. The synthesis route impacts the surface chemistry and properties of the resulting CDs. The prepared nitrogen dopped carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized via a hydrothermal method using guanidine and citric acid precursors.

The proposed probe for serum GSH determination employs a ratiometric dual-emission system consisting of N-carbon dots (blue emission at 440 nm) and an Ag-RF complex (yellow emission at 525 nm) when 370 nm is employed as excitation wavelength. Upon the addition of GSH, a complexation reaction occurs between GSH and silver in Ag-RF complex, resulting in the liberation of RF. Free RF possesses the ability to quench the fluorescence of N-CDs through Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), resulting in a reduction in the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Simultaneously, the fluorescence of RF at 525 nm starts to increase. This intricate mechanism forms the basis for the selective determination of GSH levels in human serum. The proposed fluorometric method for the determination of GSH in serum holds significant advantages over other methods, as it offers high sensitivity, selectivity, simplicity, and cost-effectiveness. Additionally, the method provides a simple and rapid analysis process, making it a valuable tool for glutathione detection in clinical settings.

Glutathione, silver nitrate, riboflavin, phosphoric acid, boric acid, glacial acetic acid, guanidine, and citric acid were acquired from Sigma-Aldrich (Steinheim, Germany). L-arginine, D-arginine, phenylalanine, histidine, aspartic acid, threonine, glycine, tryptophan, cysteine, serine, leucine, lysine, valine, proline, glucose and ascorbic acid were purchased from Fluka Analytical (New Jersey, USA). Double distilled water (18.2 MΩ.cm) was used in whole experiments. Human serum is obtained from volunteers of School Hospital in Assiut University (Assiut, Egypt).

Absorption spectra were carried out on 1601 PC UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence measurements were carried out on RF-5301PC spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Tokyo, Japan). Fourier-transform infrared spectrum (FT-IR) was collected in the range of 4000–400 cm−1 by using Nicolet™ 6700 spectrophotometer (Thermo Electron Corporation, USA) with KBr pellets. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was shown with Philips PW 1700 X-ray diffractometer. The morphology feature of N-CDs was studied by transmission electron microscopy (JEOL, Tokyo, Japan) at operating voltage of 200 kv. A laser-scattering technique (Zetasizer Nano ZS90, Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) was performed to analyze the mean diameter of the synthesized particles.

Hydrothermal approach was utilized to prepare N-CDs using citric acid and guanidine. In brief, citric acid and guanidine were combined in a 1:1 ratio by weight (1 g each) and dispersed in 25 mL of double distilled water under stirring to generate a homogeneous solution. The obtained solution was then transferred to a stainless-steel autoclave for hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 10 h. Upon cooling the autoclave to room temperature, the darkened product solution was removed and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min. This centrifugation step precipitated unwanted byproducts, allowing isolation of the N-CDs within the supernatant. The supernatant was then passed through a 0.22 μm filter to eliminate any remaining large particles. Purification and isolation of the final N-CDs was achieved by dialysis against double distilled water using a 1000 Da molecular weight cut-off membrane for 48 h. Finally, the dialyzed N-CDs solution was lyophilized to acquire solid N-CDs powder which was stored at 4 °C prior to further experimentation.

The Ag-RF complex was prepared according to previously reported procedures [18]. To prepare the RF-silver complex, an aqueous solution of RF with a concentration of 0.0001 M was adjusted to a pH of 7.0 by dropwise addition of 0.1 M sodium hydroxide. Five milliliters of 0.0002 M aqueous solution of silver nitrate were then added dropwise to 5.0 mL of the previously prepared RF solution. After complete addition of the silver nitrate, the mixture was stirred for 30 min to facilitate complexation. The formation of Ag-RF complex was visually confirmed by the appearance of a light red colored solution. The resulting complex solution was then ready for subsequent experiments.

The dual emission Ag-RF/N-CDs probe was then prepared by simply mixing equal volumes of the previously synthesized Ag-RF complex and N-CDs in aqueous solution. Upon excitation of this probe solution at 370 nm, dual fluorescence emission peaks were observed at 440 nm and 525 nm, corresponding to the emissions of the N-CD and Ag-RF components respectively.

Aqueous GSH solutions of varying concentrations (0.25–350.0 μmol/L) were prepared in double distilled water. For the assay, 1 mL aliquots of the GSH solutions were transferred into 5.0 mL volumetric flask followed by addition of 1.0 mL Britton–Robinson (B.R.) buffer (pH = 7.0) and 100 μL of a previously prepared Ag-RF complex/N-CDs probe solution. The resulting mixtures were diluted to a final volume of 5 mL using double distilled water to obtain various concentrations from 0.05 to 70.0 μmol/L. After incubation at 25 °C for 1.0 min under ambient conditions to allow complete reaction. Emission intensities were recorded at wavelengths of 440 nm and 525 nm upon excitation at 370 nm, corresponding to detection of N-CDs and the Ag-RF complex, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing of GSH in human serum samples
ce:section.ce:para: Human blood serum samples were obtained from healthy volunteers for utilization in this study. To remove serum proteins, a 1:1 vol ratio of serum and acetonitrile was combined by vortex mixing for 5 min. The deproteinized serum mixture was then filtered and dialyzed against double distilled water using a 500 Da MWCO membrane for 7 h to remove excess salts and small molecules. The filtrate was isolated by centrifugation at 8000 rpm for 10 min and passed through a 0.22 μm filter. This preprocessed serum matrix was diluted 10-fold via B.R. buffer (pH 7.0) for analysis. Various concentrations of GSH were successively added into the above treated serum to prepare a series of spiked samples (0.05–70.0 μmol/L). Fluorescence analysis was performed by mixing serum samples containing known concentrations of GSH with the Ag-RF/N-CD probe solution according to previously established measurement procedures, enabling quantification of GSH.
For detailed procedures, please refer to the supplementary data file.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structural characterization of N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and dimensions of the synthesized N-CDs were evaluated using TEM. The TEM image presented in Fig. 1 A illustrates that N-CDs exhibit a spherical shape and uniform dispersion. Analysis of particle size distribution histogram as shown in Fig. 1A inset indicates that N-CDs have diameters within a range of 1.2–6.9 nm, with an average diameter around 3.6 nm. The inset of Fig. 1A displays the high resolution TEM of N-CDs with lattice fringes spaced by 0.34 nm, corresponding to (002) diffraction plane of graphitic carbon. The crystallinity and molecular structure of N-CDs was also characterized using XRD measurements. The XRD pattern displayed in Fig. 1B shows a single broad diffraction peak centered at a 2θ angle of approximately 22.6°. The presence of only this broad peak signifies that N-CDs powder obtained is essentially amorphous, which is commonly observed for carbon dots [29]. The FT-IR spectra revealed the presence of distinct functional groups in the prepared N-CDs being investigated (Fig. 1C). The broad band observed at 3428 cm−1 can be attributed to the O-H/N-H stretching vibrations arising from the hydroxyl and amino groups. The small shoulder peak located at 2925 cm−1 corresponds to C-H stretching vibrations [30]. The sharp intense peak at 1605 cm−1 likely indicates the presence of carbonyl C = O stretching vibrations in addition to vibrations from aromatic C = C bonds. The peak at 1416 cm−1 is associated with C-H deformations from the carbon framework. The peak at 1110 cm−1 can be assigned to C-O stretching vibrations. Lastly, the low intensity peak around 628 cm−1 may be due to out-of-plane C-H bending modes [31]. The FTIR spectrum confirms successful generation of nitrogen containing carbon dots with carbonyl, carboxyl and hydroxyl moieties along with mixed graphitic and aliphatic carbon components.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of N-CDs and Ag-RF complex
ce:section.ce:para: The UV–Vis absorption spectra and fluorescence emission spectra of the N-CDs were recorded. Ultraviolet–visible absorption spectroscopy was utilized to examine the optical properties and electronic transitions for the prepared N-CDs across wavelengths from 190 to 600 nm. The obtained absorption spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks at 222 nm and 332 nm (Fig. 2 A). The shorter wavelength band is indicative of π to π* transitions localized within C = C bonds. In contrast, the broader peak centered at 332 nm can be assigned to n to π* transitions associated with C = O/C = N [32]. The emission properties of N-CDs were investigated by fluorescence spectroscopy. In Fig. 2B, it can be observed that the fluorescence intensity of the N-CDs decreases as the excitation wavelength is scanned from 340 to 430 nm. The highest fluorescence intensity is obtained when the excitation wavelength was set at 370 nm. The corresponding emission spectrum in Fig. 2A shows the N-CDs have an emission maximum centered at 440 nm when excited at 370 nm. Furthermore, the synthesized N-CDs demonstrated an appreciable fluorescence quantum yield of 33.20 %, validating their potential utility as optical probes.

XPS analysis was conducted to examine the elemental composition and verify the presence of surface functional groups in the N-CDs. The full scan XPS spectra of the N-CDs, as presented in Fig. S1A, exhibited three peaks observed at 284.03 eV, 398.31 eV, and 529.78 eV, corresponding to the binding energies of C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively. Fig. S1B illustrated the C1s spectrum, which displayed four peaks at 283.21 eV, 284.63 eV, 285.91 eV, and 287 eV, assigned to C-C, C-N, C-O, and C = O bonds, respectively. The N1s spectrum in Fig. S1C demonstrated three distinct peaks at 399.39 eV (R-NH2), 400.28 eV (pyrrolic N), and 401.39 eV (quaternary N). The O1s high core-level spectrum presented in Fig. S1D showed three peaks at 529.42 eV, 530.51 eV, and 531.81 eV, which were attributed to C-O, C = O, and O-H bonds, respectively [29].

The UV–visible absorption spectrum of RF displays characteristic peaks at 224, 269, 372, and 445 nm due to the different electronic transitions in its conjugated isoalloxazine ring system. Upon addition of silver nitrate, the intensity decreases and slight red-shifts were observed for the 269, 372, and 445 nm peaks, indicating riboflavin-silver complex formation (Fig. 2C). In contrast, the peak at 224 nm underwent significant enhancement. This can be attributed to the presence of new ligand–metal charge transfer bands arising from active coordination of riboflavin's carbonyl and imine groups with the silver center [33]. The subsequent fluorescence characterization revealed Ag-RF complex exhibits two excitation maxima at 372 nm and 460 nm. Corresponding emission scanned from these excitation wavelengths showed identical peaks centered at 525 nm (Fig. 2D). 370 nm was selected as the excitation wavelength for fluorescence measurements of both the N-CDs and Ag-RF complex as it enabled simultaneous and efficient elicitation of emissions from both sensor components.

Fluorescence stability of N-CDs under various conditions was evaluated. Fig. S2A presents the impact of solution pH on N-CD emission intensity over a pH range from 1.0 to 10.0. Negligible variation in fluorescence was detected across pH 4.0–10.0, with maximum emission exhibited at pH 7.0 in phosphate buffered solution. Under more acidic conditions below pH 4.0, fluorescence was sharply decreased indicating proton-induced quenching mechanism [34]. Consequently, a pH 7 was selected for further assays to ensure stable optimal N-CD fluorescence. Additionally, fluorescence of the N-CDs was unaffected upon exposure to high molar concentrations of sodium chloride up to 2.0 M, confirming excellent tolerance to ionic strength effects (Fig. S2B). Finally, prolonged UV irradiation only slightly decreased emission intensity after 4 h of continuous exposure, verifying minimal photobleaching effects (Fig. S2C).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of experimental conditions on GSH detection
ce:section.ce:para: A systematic optimization of analytical parameters was performed to develop a highly sensitive fluorescence method for detecting GSH using the Ag-RF/N-CD probe. The emission wavelengths of 440 nm (N-CD) and 525 nm (Ag-RF) were monitored to obtain the best ratiometric signal (F525/F440). The excitation wavelength, pH, reaction time, and volume of Ag-RF were systematically evaluated. Excitation scans indicated that 370 nm was the ideal excitation wavelength for effectively exciting both probe components. The influence of pH on the fluorescence responses ratio was investigated across a pH range of 4.0–10.0 (Fig. S3A). It was determined that using pH 7.0B.R. buffer maximized the fluorescence responses ratio. To optimize the sensor volume and reaction time, various volumes ranging from 10 to 500 μL (Fig. S3B) and reaction times of 0.5–5 min (Fig. S3C) were examined. Through screening experiments, it was determined that a sensor volume of 100 μL and a reaction time of 1 min were the optimal conditions for achieving a complete reaction and maximizing the detection performance.

The feasibility of the optimized Ag-RF/N-CD nanoprobe for ratiometric fluorescence detection of GSH was demonstrated by measuring the responses of both the 525 nm (Ag-RF) and 440 nm (N-CD) emission intensities upon introduction of varying levels of GSH (Fig. 3 A). The ratiometric fluorescence signal (F525/F440) exhibited a strong linear correlation (R2 = 0.9986) with GSH concentration ranging from 0.05 to 70.0 μmol/L based on the regression calibration plot in Fig. 3B. The corresponding linear regression equation was determined to be F525/F440 = 0.072 CGSH + 0.41. Additionally, a limit of detection (LOD) of 0.015 μmol/L GSH was achieved reflecting the sensitivity of this method. Compared to previous fluorescence-based methods for GSH detection outlined in Table 1 , the current approach offers improved sensitivity with a low limit of detection. More importantly, the ratiometric nanoprobe represents the first reported use of a dual emissive system combining Ag-RF complex and N-CDs for efficient GSH quantitation. The simple preparative procedure involving straightforward mixing of the Ag-RF fluorophore and N-CDs to form the ratiometric probe is more straightforward than multi-step covalent modifications. Unlike other reported CDs used in most prior GSH probes, the Ag-RF/N-CD component provides built-in internal reference emission for ratiometric detection, enhancing accuracy. Therefore, this work introduces a structurally novel dual emission nanoprobe that translated unique photophysical interactions between RF, Ag ions, and N-CD into a rapid, simple, and highly sensitive GSH detection method outperforming existing fluorescence techniques. Thus, ratiometric fluorescence analysis with the Ag-RF/N-CD sensor enables accurate, selective quantification of GSH across clinically relevant levels.

The selectivity of the optimized Ag-RF/N-CD ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe was investigated by exposing it to different biologically relevant amino acids, and other coexisting compounds. As shown in Fig. 4 A and B, minimal change in the F525/F440 ratio occurred upon addition of interfering species including the amino acids; L-arginine, D-arginine, phenylalanine, histidine, aspartic acid, threonine, glycine, tryptophan, cysteine, homocysteine, serine, leucine, lysine, valine and proline. Likewise, metal ions, anions, glucose and uric acid failed to induce significant responses. In contrast, the introduction of GSH (30 μmol/L) provoked enhancement in the ratiometric fluorescence signal. Minimal change in ratiometric fluorescence signal was induced upon introduction of interferents at concentrations 10-fold higher than GSH levels. The only exceptions were the biothiols cysteine and homocysteine, which showed slight responses. However, their typical abundances in biological samples are far lower than GSH [52]. Furthermore, at comparable molar ratios to GSH, these species generated negligible interferences on GSH quantitation. The selectivity arises because GSH contains two carboxyl and amine groups that enable stronger overall binding to silver ions, displacing the riboflavin ligand more efficiently than other thiols at typical biological levels. These results verify the method exhibits substantial selectivity for GSH over structurally similar amino acids and other possible interferents.

The underlying detection mechanism of the ratiometric fluorescent nanoprobe relies on competitive displacement of RF from Ag-RF complexes in the presence of GSH. The core sensing principle relies on differences in binding strength of RF compared to GSH toward silver ions [53]. Initial formation of Ag-RF complex occurs through coordination bonds between Ag+ and the ketone and nitrogen functional groups on RF. However, GSH contains higher affinity thiol and amine binding sites for Ag+ that promote dissociation of RF and formation of GSH-Ag complexes. Consequently, introduction of GSH effectively displaces the lower affinity RF ligands, liberating RF from the original Ag-RF assemblies. The free RF at 525 nm then quenches the fluorescence of the N-CDs at 440 nm. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CD when interacted with free RF was reported in many reports to be Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [54–57]. FRET is a non-radiative transfer of energy from an excited donor molecule to an acceptor molecule through dipole-dipole interaction. The efficiency of energy transfer depends on the orientation of the dipoles and the spectral overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum [57]. The UV–Vis absorbance spectrum of RF and the emission spectrum of the N-CDs show significant spectral overlap (Fig. 5 ), indicating the possibility of FRET from the N-CDs to RF. Gradual increments in GSH concentration promotes proportional dissociation of additional RF from the existing Ag-RF complex. This liberates higher quantities of RF molecules which cause more spectral overlap with the emission spectra of N-CDs (Fig. 5 inset) and consequently more fluorescence quenching of N-CDs. The presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl groups on the surface of the N-CDs facilitates hydrogen bonding with RF, reducing the spatial distance between the N-CDs and RF and further supporting the occurrence of FRET [54–57]. Experimental results confirm the existence of FRET, as the photoluminescence intensity of the N-CDs decreases while the intensity of RF increases in the mixture solution. The fluorescence quenching mechanism in the Ag-RF/N-CD system involves FRET, where the N-CDs serve as the donor and RF acts as the acceptor.

In order to gain further insights into FRET mechanism, we measured the fluorescence lifetime of Ag-RF/N-CD before and after the addition of GSH. The results, presented in Fig. S4, revealed fluorescence lifetimes of 10.51 ns and 7.79 ns, respectively. By employing Eqs. (1) to (3) [58], we calculated the donor–acceptor distance (γ). Consequently, the calculated γ between the N-CDs and RF was determined to be 11 Å within the range of 10–100 Å, which aligns with the necessary conditions for a FRET mechanism [59].

E = 1 /
[ 1 + γ /

6
]

A o
u n i t )
The fluorescence lifetime values, τ0 and τ, represent the lifetimes of Ag-RF/N-CD in the absence and presence of GSH, respectively. The orientation factor of the donor and acceptor transition dipoles, κ2, is commonly assumed to be 2/3. The quantum yield of the N-CDs is denoted as Φ, while n represents the refractive index of the medium. J(λ) corresponds to the integral of overlap values.

The efficiency of FRET, denoted as E, was calculated based on steady-state measurements using the following equation [56]:
E =
1 -
F
DA
F D
× 100 %
The FRET efficiency (E) was determined using the equation that compares the fluorescence intensity of the donor in the absence (FD) and presence (FDA) of the acceptor. In this case, the addition of GSH at a concentration of 0.05 μmol/L resulted in only a 4 % quenching of the donor fluorescence. However, when GSH concentration was increased to 10.0 μmol/L, the quenching efficiency reached approximately 38 %. This indicates that as the concentration of RF is gradually increased, the FRET efficiency from the N-CD (donor) to RF (acceptor) also increases.

To evaluate the performance of the optimized Ag-RF/N-CD ratiometric fluorescence method for GSH detection in complex biological media, human blood serum samples were analyzed. The method's high sensitivity allows for significant sample dilution rates, effectively removing potential interferences from co-existing substances. Exogenous GSH was then spiked into separate serum aliquots at 0.05, 2.0 and 6.0 μmol/L prior to analysis. As shown in Table 2 , excellent recovery rates between 96.0 and 98.5 % with relative standard deviations below 2.56 % were achieved across this clinically relevant concentration range. The accurate quantification and high precision for serum samples approaches that for standard solutions, highlighting the sensor’s resilience to matrix effects from different amino acids and other endogenous components. Thus, the developed assay enables reliable measurements of GSH in complex physiological fluids, holding great promise for practical applications ranging from medical testing to disease diagnostics.

In summary, a dual emission ratiometric fluorescence nanoprobe was successfully developed for selective and sensitive detection of glutathione (GSH) by exploiting competitive interactions with a silver-riboflavin complex. The sensor comprises a simple mixture of Ag-riboflavin displaying yellow emission and blue-fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots prepared via hydrothermal synthesis from citric acid and guanidine. Spectroscopic characterization (UV–vis absorption, IR, fluorescence) verified generation of the emissive Ag-riboflavin complex and N-doped carbon dots. TEM imaging confirmed nanoscale dimensions of the carbon dots. Upon introduction of trace glutathione, preferential coordination with silver liberates riboflavin which serves to quench the adjacent carbon dots through fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET). By ratiometrically tracking the F525/F440 emissions, sub-micromolar levels of glutathione are accurately detected across biological matrices like human serum. The sensor provides excellent linearity along with high sensitivity and selectivity. With attractive performance combined with simplicity in preparation and usage, this dual nanoprobe holds considerable potential for cost-effective and field-deployable glutathione quantification to support clinical testing and biomedical applications through routine analytical measurements.

Khalid Alhazzani: Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis. Ahmed Z. Alanazi: Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Aya M. Mostafa: Validation, Software, Resources, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. James Barker: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Software, Resources. Mohamed M. El-Wekil: Writing – original draft, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Al-Montaser Bellah H. Ali: Conceptualization, Funding acquisition, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Validation, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to extend their appreciation to the Researchers Supporting Project number (RSPD2023R593) at King Saud University, Riyadh, Saudi Arabia.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.109996.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
